excerpt: The choice of the demonstrative ⟪ar:'ذلک'⟫ (that, referring to the distant) rather than ⟪ar:'ھذہ'⟫ (this, referring to the near) invites careful reflection...suggesting that the passage refers...to another book entirely
source_location_hint: Commentary on Qur'an 2:1-2, interpretation of distal demonstrative

excerpt: ⟪ar:امیر المومنین علی علیہ السلام⟫ In his inaugural sermon upon assuming the caliphate, proclaimed from the pulpit with utmost clarity: 'Ask me before you lose me! Question me concerning all that has passed and all that shall transpire until the Day of Judgment.'
source_location_hint: Sermon 1 of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib

excerpt: The Book of which there is no doubt in its greatness is the very essence of the Prophet himself. This declaration aims to establish the authority and guardianship of the Guardian and to purify the hearts of the faithful from hesitation
source_location_hint: Discussion of tanzil and taʾwil distinction

excerpt: Among the disbelievers are those who denied the Messenger: Jews and Christians whose scriptures contained clear prophecies and tidings of the Prophet of the End-Times, with explicit commendation and his name written therein...Yet through enmity they altered these texts
source_location_hint: Section on kufr and historical denial of prophethood

excerpt: They refused obedience though aware that ⟪ar:سباقت فی الاسلام⟫ brought ʿAlī distinctive virtues, and that ⟪ar:من کنت مولاہ فھذا علی مولاہ⟫ was proclaimed before thousands at Ghadir Khumm
source_location_hint: Discussion of wilayat and succession

excerpt: Among the people of religion stands a single chief: the heart and soul of the faith. This was the Prophet in his age, and after him, his successor and the Imāms, upon whom the law rests. These are the centers of life and religion, from which all the confines of the summons and the believers draw eternal life
source_location_hint: Epistemological hierarchy and spiritual headship

excerpt: Thought that unveils monotheistic knowledge and clarifies the stations of the Messenger and Imams constitutes the heart's true work. As Allah says: 'Those who contemplate the creation of the heavens and the earth.' The Messenger taught that one moment's reflection surpasses a year of worship
source_location_hint: On contemplation (tafakkur) and knowledge

excerpt: The souls of the entire world are bound to theirs. Hence it is said: 'The death of a scholar is the death of an entire world.' Whoever slays the Prophet, messenger, or Imām from his station...has in truth murdered all of creation
source_location_hint: On the universal significance of the Imām's existence

excerpt: The removal of the self of the world—the Wasi from his station of guardianship, or the Imām from his station of leadership, or the Dāʾī from his position—constitutes murdering millions...Conversely, those who stand against usurpers and restore to the Guardian his rights deserve reward as if they had saved the lives of all creation
source_location_hint: Interpretation of Qur'an 5:32 on the universal soul

excerpt: The Messenger thus told Fāṭima: 'By Allah, you shall be humiliated after me.' Inwardly, there are orphaned, immaculate Imāms in their times, possessing no precedent. Like a ⟪ar:'دُرِّ یتیم'⟫ (orphaned pearl)—a unique jewel of incomparable worth
source_location_hint: Mystical interpretation of Fatima's station and the Imāms

excerpt: Allāh does not accept the testimony of a witness without knowledge
source_location_hint: Referenced in discussion of faith and knowledge

excerpt: Faith without knowledge is an affirmation that sickens every thinking person and invokes Allāh's displeasure
source_work: Exegetical works on hypocrisy and false belief
source_location_hint: Section on meaning of faith and intellect

excerpt: The unseen matters are rational and comprehensible to human intellect
source_location_hint: Discussion of knowledge and the rational understanding of the unseen

excerpt: Shirk in my Ummah is more hidden than the creeping of a black ant upon a black stone in the darkness of night
source_work: Prophetic tradition referenced in exegetical works
source_authority: Hadith tradition cited in Ismaili scholarship
source_location_hint: On hidden association with the divine

excerpt: The hypocrites are not truly 'people' in Allāh's sight because there is no spiritual correspondence between them and the true believers
source_location_hint: Section distinguishing two categories of humanity

excerpt: The perfect human in each age is the Prophet, the Wasi, or the Imām, and whoever draws nearer to them in knowledge deserves the name 'humanity'
source_work: Doctrinal works on Imāmology and human perfection
source_location_hint: On the nature of al-insān al-kāmil

excerpt: The person who illuminates the hidden humanity of fire is the perfect human, the guide of his age, the prophet or imām
source_work: Spiritual and allegorical exegesis of natural phenomena
source_location_hint: Parable of fire and enlightenment

excerpt: When the lights of prophethood spread and flourish through the bequest, the Wasi, and the Imāmah, Allāh withdrew from the hypocrites their share of light because of their envy and arrogance
source_work: Al-Majālis al-Mu'ayyidiyyah or related exegetical commentaries
source_location_hint: Commentary on Qur'ān 2:17 and the spiritual fate of the hypocrites

excerpt: Allāh draws the believers out of the darkness of mere words and the obscurity of figurative speech into eternal, abiding light through the Imām of the time
source_work: Exegesis of Āyah al-Kursī and related spiritual verses
source_location_hint: Discussion of inner light and the role of the Imāmate

excerpt: The earth is your mother, and she treats you with goodness
source_location_hint: On the spiritual interpretation of earth as nurturing principle

excerpt: The water that descends from the sky refers to revelation, which Allāh conveyed to the Messenger, who clothed it in the words of the Qur'ān and brought it down to the earth of the guardian, the successor
source_work: Taʾwīl works on revelation and succession
source_location_hint: Section on spiritual water and the transmission of knowledge through the Imāms

excerpt: Physical water cannot remove the filth of Satan; revelation of physical waters alone does not increase love in hearts or transform enemies into friends
source_location_hint: Discussion distinguishing outward and inward purification

excerpt: The scholar of ta'wīl draws out the truths and meanings hidden in the words of the Qur'ān and informs believers of true monotheism and the knowledge of the Qur'ān's origin
source_work: Works on ta'wīl and esoteric exegesis
source_location_hint: On the function of the Wasi and exegetes in revealing Qur'ānic mysteries

excerpt: upon whom the Messenger of Allah opened a thousand chapters of knowledge, and from every chapter a thousand more were unlocked
source_location_hint: multiple majalis on Quranic interpretation and Imamic knowledge

excerpt: Those who spoke before – Salman al-Farsi, Miqdad ibn al-Aswad, Abu Dharr al-Ghaffari, Ammar Yasir, Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr
source_location_hint: discussion of the Companions and their spiritual stations

excerpt: Angels are the people of obedience; they perform what they are commanded and execute what they are ordained
source_location_hint: discussion of cosmic orders and obedience

excerpt: the Qur'an's inner dimension is the soul; the outward is mere form – a sculptor may craft beauty but cannot breathe life
source_location_hint: taʾwil of Quranic verses on apparent and hidden meanings

excerpt: Three parties shall taste the heat of prophethood's fire: disbelievers like Muʿawiya and Yazid; those reciting the Qur'an yet blind to meaning; and those claiming Shi'a while believing like the Nasibi
source_location_hint: majlis addressing disbelief and exaggeration in allegiance

excerpt: The saints' inner service continues though their outward stations are usurped; they move quietly about their affairs though you perceive it not
source_work: Rawdat al-Mustafid (attributed to Husayn ibn Jadwan)
source_location_hint: exegesis of martyrdom and spiritual station

excerpt: Wa-ma yaʿlamu taʾwīlahu illā Allāh wa-l-rāsighūn fī al-ʿilm yaqūlūn āmannā bih kullun min ʿindu rabbinā
source_location_hint: Sūrat al-ʿImrān

excerpt: Allāh is the witness, along with the angels and those possessed of knowledge, that there is no god but He, establishing justice
source_location_hint: Sūrat al-ʿImrān

excerpt: Sawā nurīhim āyātinā fī l-āfāq wa-fī anfusihim hattā yatabayyan lahum annahu l-haqq
source_location_hint: Sūrat Fuṣṣilat

excerpt: Inna awwal bayt wuḍiʿa li-l-nās lallādhī bi-Bakka mubārakan wa-hudān li-l-ʿālamīn
source_location_hint: Sūrat al-ʿImrān

excerpt: The Commander of the Faithful upon him be peace said: I am the point beneath the letter ba; whoever recognizes this point recognizes its reality
source_work: Attributed to ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib, echoing Fatimid esoteric commentary traditions
source_author: Imam ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib (first Imam)
source_location_hint: Ismaili taʾwīl sources

excerpt: The Anwār gathered on Friday and dispersed on Saturday; the Prophet gathered all lights in ʿAlī, who transmitted them to the Imams
source_work: Fatimid esoteric doctrinal tradition, also reflected in Mu'ayyad al-Shirazi and Kirmani works
source_location_hint: Commentary traditions on Qur'anic signs and the Imamate

excerpt: Muḥammad and Kaʿb both equal 92 in numerological reckoning (ʿilm al-jummal)
source_location_hint: Numerological commentary on the Kaʿba and Muḥammad

excerpt: The heavens are the feast of the established; the earth is the feast of believers. Darkness is outward invitation; light is inward
source_work: Attributed to Mawlānā Ghulām Ḥussain, reflecting Ismaili interpretive methodology
source_author: Ismaili theological tradition (possibly cited through Nakhbat al-Kalām or related sources)
source_location_hint: Commentary on Qur'an 6:1

excerpt: Inna alladhīna kadhdhabhū bi-āyātinā wa-stankharū ʿanhā lā tuftaḥu lahum abwābu al-samāʾ
source_location_hint: Sūrat al-Aʿrāf

excerpt: Those whom people invoke besides Allah lack power of intercession except those who bear witness to truth and know it
source_work: Qur'an 43:86; also Fatimid esoteric understanding of Imamic authority
source_author: revealed to Muhammad (pbuh) / Ismaili exegetical tradition
source_authority: canonical text and Ismaili doctrinal sources
source_location_hint: Sūrat al-Zukhruf and related Imami commentaries

excerpt: We made the son of Mary and his mother a sign, and gave them refuge on a mound
source_authority: canonical Islamic text and Ismaili exegesis
source_location_hint: Sūrat al-Muʾminūn

excerpt: The saints of heaven are Allah's holy ones, majestic and noble, whose states affect the soul; their gates are opened to believers
source_work: Ismaili doctrinal commentary on spiritual hierarchies and inner dimensions
source_location_hint: Commentary on Qur'an 7:40 and the meanings of heaven in taʾwīl

excerpt: The camel represents the arrogant one; the tailor is he who prepares the garment of piety; the needle is the subtle point of monotheism
source_work: Attributed to Mu'ayyad al-Shirazi or Kirmani in Ismaili esoteric commentary; also reflects in Majālis al-Muʾayyadiyya
source_author: Ismaili doctrinal tradition, possibly Mu'ayyad al-Shirazi or related authorities
source_location_hint: Exegesis of Qur'an 7:40 in Majālis

excerpt: O children of Adam, let not Satan deceive you as he expelled your parents from Paradise, stripping them of their garments
source_location_hint: Sūrat al-Aʿrāf

excerpt: We do not abrogate a verse or cause it to be forgotten, but We bring one better than it or similar to it
source_location_hint: Sūrat al-Baqara

excerpt: If you call them to guidance, they hear not; you see them looking at you, yet they see not
source_location_hint: Sūrat al-Aʿrāf

excerpt: Have not the disbelievers seen that the heavens and the earth were one mass, which We tore asunder, and We made from water every living thing
source_location_hint: Sūrat al-Anbiyāʾ

excerpt: When We will a thing to be, We say to it: Be, and it is
source_location_hint: Sūrat al-Nahl

excerpt: The inner heaven is the Prophet; the inner earth is his guardian. The Prophet imparts knowledge to his legatee, who nourishes believers' souls eternally
source_work: Ismaili doctrinal commentary on spiritual cosmology and Imamic authority
source_author: Ismaili theological tradition, likely reflected in works attributed to Mu'ayyad al-Shirazi
source_location_hint: Esoteric commentary on Qur'an 21:30 and the relationship of the Prophet to his successor

excerpt: Everything whose existence is eternal and fixed is the same and that is God... this religion has been present in all times and has been brought by all the prophets
source_location_hint: on the eternity of the true religion

excerpt: Since we have learned and understood Islam or faith from the Messenger of Allah... the Messenger of Allah was in fact the cause of Allah
source_location_hint: on the Messenger as intermediary of divine knowledge

excerpt: There is no way to know the true meaning of the Creator... He made him know His face, i.e. the perfect religion and the knowledge of the Blessed One, through which he was blessed with saints, prophets, angels, the Throne, the Chair, Paradise
source_location_hint: on God's grace in granting knowledge of divine unity to humanity

excerpt: Orbit refers to the shape of a circle. No planet can get out of its sky. In the same way, every stage of the da'wah has a circle in which he performs his duties. It is not permissible for a person to do anything beyond the authority of his position
source_work: instructional text on cosmic order and the summons
source_location_hint: on the stations and limits of the summoners in the invitation to faith

excerpt: Once upon a time, the people of Medina came to the court of the Prophet with bags full of jewels... The Prophet asked only for love of the Ahl al-Bayt. This shows that the love of the Ahl al-Bayt is greater than all the wealth, beauty and government of the world
source_work: Majālis (Assembly teachings on reward of prophethood)
source_location_hint: on the supreme importance of love for the Prophet's family

excerpt: Allah has created this vast universe out of love for the five of them... Fātimah Zahrāʾ was born, that is why she is called Mashiyat Allah (the will of Allah)
source_work: prophetic traditions and Ismaili exegesis on creation and the five pure ones
source_location_hint: on the universe created through love of Panjtan Pak

excerpt: When the Messenger of Allah completed his preaching, he waited for the people to pay the reward themselves; But the Messenger of Allah despaired and was commanded by Allaah to ask for the reward of his Prophethood
source_location_hint: on the timing and reason for the Prophet's request for recompense

excerpt: Everything is not permissible without reward and compensation... Every command of the Shariah is a part of the propagation of the Messenger of Allah. If you are not rewarded for it, no act of Shariah will be acceptable
source_work: legal and theological instruction on the necessity of compensation
source_location_hint: on reward as the condition of legality in religious acts

excerpt: Before the Messenger of Allah, 124,000 prophets came, but no one asked for the reward of their prophethood... At the time of the Messenger of Allah, the religion of Islam was perfected, its laws were codified... the work of his prophethood was completed and he asked for his reward
source_location_hint: on why only the final prophet requested recompense

excerpt: His prior transgression consisted in his inclination towards Abu Bakr... But it was the decision of nature that Abu Bakr should not be given even a place in the invitation of the inner world. Therefore, Gabriel came with this warning to the Prophet
source_location_hint: on the Prophet's inclination and divine correction regarding inner authority

excerpt: The Messenger, upon whom Allah bestowed throughout his life the titles of honor—Muzammil, Muddaththir, Ṭāhā, Yāsīn—is here addressed as 'O Messenger!' This constituted a most stern rebuke
source_work: taʾwīl of Qurʾānic verse 5:67 and surrounding context
source_location_hint: on the severity of Allah's command regarding the Ghadir proclamation

excerpt: In this verse, Allah forgives the veil of Muhammad for these two sins... the delay in announcing Ali's will... and his inclination towards Abu Bakr
source_location_hint: on divine forgiveness of the Prophet's two lapses

excerpt: We imagine the soul to be singular, yet upon reflection we discover three dimensions within it... The lowest degree of the soul manifests as vegetation... the animal soul perceive the world through the senses... the rational soul, the human faculty
source_work: mystical and psychological teaching on the three souls
source_author: Syedna Ghulām Ḥusayn Sahib transmitting the Sultan of the Knowers
source_location_hint: on the threefold nature of the human soul and its stations

excerpt: none can touch the Qur'an except those who are purified...the touching (*yamass*) mentioned here does not denote outward, physical contact, but rather the inward comprehension of meaning
source_location_hint: interpretation of Q. 56:79

excerpt: the soul (*rūḥ*) constitutes the true reality within the human being...the soul does not undergo the vicissitudes that afflict the body, but remains steadfast and immutable
source_location_hint: doctrine of form and soul analogy in Quranic exegesis

excerpt: I am leaving two precious things among you—the Book of Allah and my Progeny...There shall be no separation between them...no severance between the Qur'an and the Ahl al-Bayt
source_work: Various Ismaili sources citing the hadith al-thaqalayn
source_location_hint: doctrine of twin authority of Qur'an and Ahl al-Bayt

excerpt: ten stages through which forms progress and attain human perfection (*kamāl insānī*)...the ten levels...Mineral Strength to Power of Humanity
source_location_hint: interpretation of Q. 89:1-4 (Surat al-Fajr)

excerpt: Allah Himself concerns Himself with the Prophet's satisfaction...so long as the Messenger of Allah is pleased, the bestowal of Allah's blessings will continue without cessation
source_author: al-Muʾayyad al-Shīrāzī or Sayyidnā Ḥasan Khān
source_location_hint: interpretation of Q. 93:5 and 33:56

excerpt: the Companions of the Prophet approached him...what of our brothers who drank wine abundantly and died in that state, before the Lord forbade it to us?
source_authority: Ismaili da'wa response on divine mercy
source_location_hint: answer to questions on foregoing prophets and believers

excerpt: the Imāms of the Qāʾim's period surpass the Imāms of the Ibrāhīmī period...the succession of Imāms preceding the Prophet was called the Ābbā (the fathers), following him...called the Abnāʾ (the sons)
source_work: Lessons of the Prophet or Jamiʿ al-Hikmatayn
source_author: Sayyidnā Ḥusayn ibn Jadīn or Nasir-i Khusraw
source_location_hint: interpretation of higher meanings of Sura 93

excerpt: the people of Moses refused to enter the gate—they denied their successor Aaron—and they corrupted the word...into ⟪ar:'حطۃ'⟫, so were they punished
source_location_hint: interpretation of Q. 2:58 and 2:189 on gates and doors

excerpt: the word birr (*righteousness*) at the beginning of this verse and the word taqwā (*piety*) in the middle have no connection whatsoever to one who enters an ordinary brick-and-lime dwelling
source_work: Kitab al-ʿAlim wa-l-Ghulam or Asas al-Taʾwil
source_author: Jaʿfar ibn Mansur al-Yaman or Qadi al-Nuʿman
source_location_hint: esoteric exegesis of 'piety' and spiritual entry

excerpt: the Messenger Adam came from the upper dimension of humanity; Noah came from the left; Abraham from behind; Moses from below (confronting Adam); Jesus from the right (confronting Noah); and Muḥammad al-Muṣṭafā came from the front
source_location_hint: doctrine of six messengers aligned to six dimensions of man

excerpt: the Messengers or Nāṭiq (speakers) explained the outward actions, while those who explained their meanings were the Asās (foundations) or Waṣī (successors)
source_authority: Ismaili doctrine of speaker-prophet and successor
source_location_hint: typology of Nāṭiq and Asās in prophethood

excerpt: During the lifetime of the Nāṭiq (the Speaker-Prophet), his successor is called Asās (the Foundation). After the Nāṭiq's death, he is designated Waṣī (the Executor-Successor). Muʿallim ʿAlī was the Asās during the lifetime of the Messenger of Allah, and after the Prophet's death, he became the Waṣī
source_location_hint: role of the successor in each prophetic cycle
